**Watch out for temporarily active geographical zones, e.g., set out at the requirement of the military or over aerodromes belonging to aeroclubs. Such zones activate on specific days and times or at the request of an authority applying for the zone.**

Imagine a situation – you are piloting a drone, and a “military” zone, exactly at your location, suddenly pops up. What should you do in such a situation? **You should end the flight and your check-in as soon as possible.**

**How to avoid such situation? When planning the flight and checking zones, remember to take into account the inactive zones as well, and check their planned activities. Remember also that a small number of zones can appear suddenly and without prior planning. Track the information available to remote pilots through tools assisting unmanned flights.**

> [!abstract] Fun Fact
> When shooting Episode VIII of the “Star Wars”, Disney used drones to … combat paparazzi drones.

---
## Before the first flight

### UAS operator – responsibilities, certificates, formalities

**An unmanned aerial system operator means any legal or natural person operating or intending to operate one or more UAV.**  

1. The operator must be registered in the ULC system – you have already done that.
2. After registering, an individual operator number will be generated for you and you must affix it to your UAV in a place where it can be seen. In exceptional situations (e.g. when your UAV is a realistically reproduced model), it is allowed to affix the number on the battery compartment.
3. Where a company is the UAV operator – every remote pilot who performs flights with company drones must have relevant competencies and use the operator (company) number.
4. The operator number must also be introduced to the UAV software (if it has such a function).
5. You must develop operating procedures (if you employ more than one remote pilot) and designate a specific remote pilot (pilots) to conduct a given operation (it is important to make it clear who is the person responsible for each flight).
6. Prior to the flight, make sure that there is not radio interference which could affect the drone's command and control link.
7. It is your responsibility that the UAV remote pilot and personnel operating the drone read the UAV instruction manual and your procedures and receive information about geographical zones. Make sure that these persons are aware of the threats related to A1 and A3 category operations.
8. If your personal data has changed, you have 14 days to edit it or submit any changes to the data you provided in the system.

> [!info] Remember
> Flying a drone without an affixed operator number = a fine.

### UAS operator – responsibilities, certificates, formalitie

**The pilot of an unmanned aerial vehicle – a person flying the drone.**

Before the flight:

1. You must obligatorily complete training and pass exams required for the type of operation in which you will be involved.
2. You should have up-to-date information about all geographical zones published by the Polish Air Navigation Services Agency.
3. Check for obstacles and presence of persons who are not involved in operating the UAV.
4. Check whether your drone is airworthy and the controller is working properly.

> [!danger] Note!
> You don’t have to be 14 to pilot a drone, if:
> - your drone is a toy.
> - you built it yourself, for own use, and it weighs up to 250 g
> - you are flying under direct supervision of a UAV pilot who is 14 years of age and hold relevant qualifications.

During an open category flight:

1. It is not allowed to operate UAVs under the influence of psychoactive or hallucinogenic substances, alcohol, or during sickness.
2. You must keep your UAV at a distance which allows you to see it clearly. If you want to perform an FPV flight, you must be accompanied by an observer who will verify the airspace and immediately inform you about obstacles noticed, and give instructions to land immediately.
3. If you or your observer notice a manned aeroplan, you must give way to it and make sure that you are far away from it.
4. If you have any doubts regarding your operation, you should land immediately.
5. Operate your UAV in accordance with the manufacturer instruction manual.
6. Follow the operator procedures (if you are one – you cannot change them voluntarily).
7. Don't fly where rescue service operations are in progress (if you see an accident, keep away from the site because it may become necessary to use a medical rescue helicopter);
8. Remember to observe geographical zone restrictions.

**A good practice for the Pilot (and a potential observer) is to wear a reflective vest that will distinguish the person among bystanders or services. However, it is not an obligation in relation to the entire open category.**

> [!danger] Note!
> - There is no such thing as a ‘private airspace’ – e.g., if your meadow is located within a currently active DRA-P zone, you cannot fly over it.
> - Piloting a drone that weighs more than 250 g ALWAYS requires appropriate UAV pilot's permissions, even if only taking photos over your own land.
> - Remember that the fines in different European Union countries for failure to observe the law for Operators and Pilots may differ from these in Poland. However, always follow the legislation applicable in a given country.

### Third party liability insurance – do I need it?

In the European Union and in Poland, **third-party liability cover is obligatory only for drones weighing 250 grams or more**. Every EU Member State may introduce obligatory third-party liability insurance for lighter drones at its own discretion. However, we suggest that you buy such insurance regardless of the mass of your UAV.

> [!danger] Note!
> - Regularly check the [www.ulc.gov.pl](https://elearning.uav.pansa.pl/www.ulc.gov.pl) website not to miss any legislation changes.
> - When flying in a different country – always check the [EASA website](https://www.easa.europa.eu/en/domains/civil-drones/naa) for any required insurance.

> [!info] Fun Fact
> Google has already invested more than USD 5 MM in a system for protecting endangered species against poachers. It is based on a network of drones monitoring herds, nesting sites and watering places.


### GDPR – do I need to care?

**You absolutely do**, especially if your drone is able to take photographs or make video.

1. If you decide to gather and process personal data (including for professional purposes), read about your responsibilities arising from the GDPR.
2. If you are requested to erase personal data, you must do so immediately. You do not need to erase personal data only if:  
    - retention of the data is a legal obligation (e.g. when you have recorded an accident or a crime);
    - the personal data held by your company (organization) is necessary for exercising the right to freedom of expression;
    - for reasons of public interest (e.g. public health or scientific, statistical or historical research).

**Personal data is any information related to an identified or identifiable natural person.** The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a law applicable in the European Union. It contains provision on the protection of natural persons in relation to personal data processing and provisions on free exchange of such data (OJ L EU L 119 of 4 May 2016, p. 1, as am.).

> [!abstract] Remember
> Even if you are not recording videos or taking photos, flying over someone’s premises can be a nuisance and violate privacy! Prior to taking off, provide the owners and persons within a given area with planned flight information. You will avoid unpleasant situations, accusations or even drone destruction. 
> Ethical and responsible drone piloting also includes respecting the privacy of bystanders and their property!

### Remote pilot preparation prior to the flight

A decision concerning take-off should always be preceded by a brief analysis - are you in an appropriate mental and physical condition to perform the flight safely? If you have a driving licence, would you decide to drive a car in your current condition? Pay particular attention to such factors as:

- **Sickness** - resign from the flight if you are sick, have fever or visual problems;
- **Medications** - do not fly if you are taking medications that affect your mental and physical abilities;
- **Mental fitness and emotions** - stress, depression, fears can affect, among other things, concentration, reflexes, anticipation ability;
- **Fatigue** - be well-rested and have a good sleep before you start piloting a UAV;
- **Alcohol and other intoxicants** - never start a flight if you have drunk alcohol or taken intoxicants.

> [!info] Fun Fact
> Jednymi z pierwszych i najczęściej używanych BSP w historii lotnictwa, były drony turbośmigłowe Ryan Firebee. Do służby w Siłach Powietrznych USA trafiły już w 1951 r.

### UAV instruction manual – do not ignore it!

The instruction manual should contain the following information:

- a description of the main elements of the system and its preparation to work;
- the rules on controlling the drone and possibility of changing them (learn to recognise and understand how individual buttons, levers and switches on the UAV controller work);
- a list of functions and equipment that affect flight safety;
- emergency and flight support mode settings;
- a manual for installing the app in a smartphone or tablet;
- a process of updating the software;
- a procedure for entering the individual operator number to the system (if the drone has such an option, RID – Remote ID);
- safety considerations relating to securing the payload and additional equipment, avoiding injuries from propellers or other sharp edges, storing and using the batteries, required maintenance activities;
- system capabilities and limitations - the maximum permitted take-off mass , the maximum forward speed and climb rate,
- environmental limitations - the maximum wind speed, resistance to rain and humidity, the temperature range for proper operation, ability to automatically evade obstacles and other automatic modes, resistance to interference.

**All the data above should be memorized. Drones should not be flown without the pilot knowing the data! In such a case you risk the safety and health of yourself, bystanders, animals!**

> [!abstract] Remember
> The camera is not intended for recording breathtaking landscapes only! A drone fitted with a camera should also help avoiding a collision with other aircraft and obstacles.

### Mission planning

**Prior to every drone flight, it is a good practice to prepare the checklist.**

1. The flight path plotted on a map, with verification of geographical zones and strategic facilities.
2. Defined take-off and landing sites. Ideally, they should be on hardened, non-sandy surface. Airborne sand and dust can pollute the motor of your UAV leading to its failure. Motor failure in flight is very dangerous and certainly impacts the safety of the UAV you are piloting.
3. The maximum range of your UAV plotted on a map. Note – the range depends on weather conditions, among other things. If you finish your flight at the take-off site, remember that you must have enough power to return!
4. Changes in terrain elevation Remember – you are allowed to fly up to a maximum distance of 120 m between the UAV and the nearest point on the ground.
5. Marked obstacles which can limit the visibility of the UAV by the pilot (you). When planning a flight, you cannot allow losing sight of your UAV – such a flight will no longer be safe and will violate open-category regulations!
6. An analysis whether any uninvolved persons may appear on the flight path. Remember, that in such a case, you must evade them as quickly as possible while maintaining a safe distance.
7. Marked sources of electromagnetic interference, e.g. antenna masts (including BTS – mobile network masts), power lines, Wi-Fi routers, high steel structures. Also pay attention to magnetic storms forecast in the near future.
8. Established rules and method of communication with an observer (if involved in the mission).

**If you intend to participate in drone races, remember to do so through official clubs and modelling associations. It is also best to get familiar with FPV (First Person View) flights or, in other words, “drone-view” control, as part of their activities.**

> [!danger] Note!
> Prior to each take-off of your UAV, check-in, and report the completion of each flight afterwards. This enables real-time transmission of information on your activities to Air Traffic Services (ATS) you are already familiar with.
>**Always plan your flight to mitigate the nuisance for persons not involved in UAV and animals!**

### Do you want to fly safely? Pay attention to these factors!

**Check the weather forecast in two independent sources, at three days and one before the flight and on the day of the flight. The final decision regarding the start of your UAV should be made immediately prior to the air operation, based on the weather situation at the location of the planned flight.**

Things that you need to take into account:

| Wind speed and direction          | Temperature                                 | KP index     |
| --------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ------------ |
| Probability of precipitation /fog | Possibility of thunderstorms with lightning | GNSS jamming |

**Wind strength and direction** – the maximum wind speed is specified by the manufacturer in the user manual for your UAV, and exceeding this value is NOT PERMITTED. Take the wind direction into account when flying your drone at every stage of the flight. Remember that the drone flies relative to the air, and wind is a symptom of the movement of air masses.

**Temperature** – the manufacturer has also specified the parameter range (minimum/maximum) in the user manual for your UAV. Exceeding the specified range is not permitted. Temperature also affects the remote control itself – do you take this into account when planning your flights? Don't forget about it.

**KP index** – a numerical value that determines the likelihood of electromagnetic radiation interference. It is directly related to solar activity and can disrupt communication systems and drone positioning systems. The higher the value of this index, the greater the likelihood of interference with your UAV.

**Precipitation and fog affect visibility**, the UAV's flight capability (e.g. resistance to precipitation – or lack thereof – specified in your drone's operating manual, or the so-called laminar flow of the wing/propeller) and the comfort of the unmanned pilot during flight – do you like flying while standing in the rain?

**Storm** – an atmospheric phenomenon involving electromagnetic discharges that can disrupt or even damage your UAV, which can lead to potentially dangerous situations that you should avoid during flight. Often associated with additional heavy precipitation. If a storm is forecast – postpone your flight to a different date if possible.

**Transmission jamming (RC link)** – this is the deliberate disruption of communication between the controller and the drone. It can cause the drone to suddenly start behaving unpredictably or you may lose contact with it.

**GNSS jamming** – this is the deliberate interference with GNSS signals, which causes a drone to partially or completely lose visibility of satellites, ultimately causing the drone to ‘lose’ its position.

> [!danger] Note!
> Check if your drone has a flight mode without GNSS signal (e.g. ATTI mode). Practise turning it on and controlling it in this mode. Remember that the drone may not maintain a steady position and may drift with the wind.  
Flying with the GNSS system turned off requires greater piloting skills. You can acquire these skills by practising under the supervision of a professional instructor.

GNSS spoofing – this is a technique in which someone impersonates a GNSS signal by sending false location data to a drone. Instead of the real signal from the satellites, the drone receives a fabricated signal that looks real.

> [!danger] Note!
> How does spoofing work in practice?  
The drone thinks it is in a different location than it actually is. This can lead to a change in flight path, landing in an unauthorised location and loss of control by the pilot, who sees incorrect data on the screen.


> [!danger] Note!
> Why is spoofing more dangerous than jamming?  
Jamming causes signal loss – the drone knows that something is wrong.  
Spoofing is insidious – the drone does not know that it is being deceived, so it can perform dangerous actions.


> [!abstract] Remember
> Understanding how your drone works and its positioning system will help you reduce the chances of it behaving erratically.  
Never fully trust your equipment – it is just a machine and can always fail.

### Check your UAV before the flight!

Prior to flying you must absolutely check the following:


| Visibility of the operator's number on your drone. | Charging the control device and additional devices (tablet, phone).                      | Charging, temperature, and condition of the drone's batteries.       | Propeller attachment and their proper rotation.                      |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Condition of the airframe - no cracks, dents.      | Engine condition - no backlash, free rotation, in line with the engine's characteristics | Fuselage condition - completeness, lack of damage, screw tightening. | Fuselage condition - completeness, lack of damage, screw tightening. |


| Operation of the green position light - for flights before dawn and after dusk. | Quality of remote control link - ensure no interference at the takeoff location. | Compass calibration - wait for the drone to position itself and check for any interference. |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Video transmission quality - especially during FPV flights.                     | Fail-safe function programming - system behavior in case of signal loss.         | Go Home function programming - automatic return to the takeoff location.                    |
> [!abstract] Remember
> Every device can break down. Even large aircraft crash due to technical causes. Therefore, you should not blindly trust a device which you have just taken out of the box. Your flight should be planned so that it does not do any harm to property or people in the event of failure.

---
### Training and test flights

**Drone pilots should be responsible, aware of their actions and able to respond to failures and unexpected situations.**

  

Continuous improvement of skills and regular practice are critical to the pilot's development and preservation of a high level of flight safety and quality. You should therefore **start with a series of test flights**.

1. Start from simple manoeuvres like take-off, landing, maintaining flight stability, rotating your drone, until you gain confidence in piloting. Avoid fast and sudden manoeuvres, especially at the beginning of the learning process!   
2. Gradually extend test flights by adding more advanced manoeuvres like flying in a straight line, changing the direction and speed of flight.  
3. Perform your flights in a controlled manner and in open space:
    - choose areas with low traffic, far from busy roads, assemblies of people and other obstacles;
    - practice precision piloting, maintaining a constant height and flight smoothness.
4. Practice flights in various weather conditions:
    - at the beginning – under the supervision of an experienced remote pilot;
    - learn how to respond to adverse weather conditions, e.g. how to perform a safe landing.
5. Simulate emergencies and learn how to respond to them:
    - practice only in remote areas without any buildings, trees etc., outside restricted zones and under the supervision of an experienced remote pilot;
    - practice emergency procedures: lost radio signal, low battery power, technical problems;
    - learn automatic modes, including Return to Home (RTH);
    - perform a manual emergency landing in a safe place.
### Basic principles of flying drones safely in the Open category

1. **No regulation can replace common sense and thinking.**
2. **You must always see your UAV in flight**. The exception is a flight with an observer – then the drone must be seen by at least one person.
3. Remember – **the maximum drone flight height is 120 m** (above ground level, taking into account terrain features, e.g., hills, mountains).
4. If you must fly over an obstacle which is higher than 120 m, you may fly within 15 metres above the obstacle's height, but only at the explicit approval of the obstacle owner (e.g. an agreement with the owner concerning an inspection); the maximum horizontal distance from the obstacle is 50 m.
5. **Keep your UAV at a safe distance from uninvolved persons, do not fly over assemblies of people (horizontal distance from an assembly of people shall not be lower than the current flight height).**
6. **Control the battery power level** (fuel reserve).
7. A drone is not intended for carrying **hazardous materials** (pursuant to the regulations, hazardous materials include articles or substances that – in the event of an incident or accident – may pose a threat to health, safety, property or the environment) or **dropping** any objects!
8. Always **give way to manned aircraft**!
9. If you notice another UAV, it is best to reduce the speed and fly away from it.
10. **Do not fly a drone close to an area where an emergency response effort is ongoing** (unless you are authorised by competent services to do so).
11. In the event of failure, **do not panic**. Follow the UAV instruction manual!
12. **If you lose link with your drone**, do not panic, the **Fail-Safe mode** should kick in. If you still have no communication and the drone does not execute the emergency procedure automatically - **get closer to it while turning the antennas towards the UAV.**
13. **If you discover a malfunction of the components, bring the UAV back to you immediately**, or – in the event of a serious defect – land as soon as possible (check if an emergency landing is possible).
14. **If you have completely lost control of your drone** and it is moving uncontrollably posing a threat to third parties, **try to abort the flight** by turning off the propulsion and causing the UAV to fall down in a safe place.
15. Abort the flight as soon as you notice that it poses a threat to other aircraft, people, animals, environment or property

> [!abstract] Remember
> - ther aircraft, such as helicopters of the Polish Medical Air Rescue can be encountered even at an height below 120 m. A small drone may pose a deadly risk to the crew and passengers of such a helicopter or manned aircraft.
> -EU and domestic regulations do not precisely stipulate the principles of flying inside buildings. You can conduct such a flight but – as always – remember about safety and particular attention, as well as obtain a permission for such a flight from a building administrator.

### Automatic modes

1. Selected types of automatic modes:
    - automatic take-off and landing;
    - automatic return to the take-off sie (Return To Home / Return To Launch);
    - automatic flight in the event of loss of the remote control signal (Fail Safe)
    - automatic detection and avoidance of obstacles (Obstacle Avoidance);
    - possibility to fly along designated points (Waypoints);
    - possibility to follow a moving base station / remote pilot Follow Me);
    - fly around a selected object (Point of Interest).
2. During automatic flight, the remote pilot should always be able to take manual control of the UAV.
3. Before using automatic modes, read the user manual!
4. Perform a test flight with the use of automatic modes – in a vast open space, ideally with the support of a more experienced remote pilot.
5. During a test flight, learn how to quickly terminate automatic flight (in case of a dangerous or emergency situation). The fastest method of taking back manual control is described by the manufacturer in the instruction manual.
6. In the event of loss of control of the drone, the manual execution of the FailSafe mode by the remote pilot will very often save the situation. If you have it, it is the best way of recovering from a difficult situation. You are giving yourself time to think about your next steps. Remember that the emergency function (FailSafe) executed by the pilot can always be aborted and full manual control of the UAV can be gained.

**It’s worth having a drone with a default FailSafe function**. In the event of a failure of communication loss, these provide the opportunity to safely return to the take-off point. And if you become really dragged into flying drones, you might want to consider purchasing a model with systems to determine:

- basic location, which is the current location, flight speed, height and direction;
- emergency location, which is the last location of your drone in an emergency or communication loss situation.

**Remember that these systems are required when flying under the ‘specific’ category, beyond the field of view range.**

### Remote pilot checks and penalties

We assume that after the course and exam you will remember about safety, maintain particular caution and fly responsibly.

However, you should know who and how may check you while you are conducting a UAV operation:

- authorized employees of the Civil Aviation Authority (Aviation Supervision Inspection);
- officers of the Internal Security Agency, the Foreign Intelligence Agency, the Military Counterintelligence Service, the Military Intelligence Service, the Central Anti-Corruption Bureau, the Police, the Border Guard, the State Protection Service, the National Revenue Administration, the Prison Service, the Marshal's Guard, inspectors of the Internal Supervision Bureau, Professional soldiers assigned to positions in the Military Counterintelligence Service or the Military Intelligence Service, soldiers of the Military Gendarmerie, and members of the Armed Forces of the Republic of Poland;
- municipal (city) guards;
- forest rangers – in forest areas;
- national Park Rangers – in national parks;
- officers of the State Fire Service – in cases where the operation being conducted may pose a threat to the safety of State Fire Service officers engaged in firefighting and rescue operations or performing other official duties.

All those services have access to the UAV operator registry and can quickly verify your privileges. And in the event of violating air traffic regulations, the fines will be imposed pursuant to the [Aviation Law Act](https://www.ulc.gov.pl/pl/prawo/prawo-krajowe/ustawa-prawo-lotnicze-i-akty-wykonawcze/4851-ustawa-prawo-lotnicze).
  
**What if you have crashed your UAV or lost sight of it, and you cannot find the wreckage?**  

Since 1 February 2023, aviation occurrences can be reported (also anonymously) through [ECCAIRS](https://e2.aviationreporting.eu/reporting). Detailed information on this system can be found at the CAA website, in the [‘reporting air incidents'](https://ulc.gov.pl/pl/powiadamianie-o-zdarzeniach-lotniczych).

To report air traffic services about in-flight UAV issues (e.g., drone escape), follow the procedures published by the PANSA. **The report is not intended to impose consequences on the operator or pilot, but rather to increase operational awareness by air traffic services and utilize the acquired information in the process of ensuring aviation safety."**

> [!danger] Note!
> Always be aware of the consequences behind violating regulations and taking responsibility (including penal!) for illegal activities or mistakes.

### After the flight - tips

- Turn off the system.
- Check the temperature of the electric engines – is any of them clearly hotter than the others?
- Check the general condition of the UAV, in particular its structural elements and screws.
- Check the propellers and batteries for any damage.
- Prepare the system for transport – keep the batteries outside the drone.
- Load the batteries to the level dedicated for storage (see the UAV instruction manual).
- At the CAA website you can find a [guide on carrying a UAV and its accessories on a plane](https://www.ulc.gov.pl/_download/informacje/drony/dron-samolot-sloneczne-internet.pdf).

> [!danger] Note!
> A battery left unattended in a charger may self-ignite and cause a severe, hard-to-extinguish fire.

### Photo and video

Here are a few hints on UAV photography and videography. You will surely find more online – on blogs, vlogs and social media chat groups. But:

1. Learn (ideally in practice) the basic techniques of taking photos and shooting films with a UAV:
    - composition and framing of photos and catching interesting images from different perspectives;
    - different photo and video camera settings, such as white balance, sharpness, exposure modes.
2. Learn the details of your device to record high quality video materials:
    - experiment with different camera settings, such as definition, shutter speed and ISO value;
    - use the image stabilisation function to ensure smooth and stable images.
3. Remember about safety during such practices – perform test flights in remote areas, outside flight-restriction zones. Remember also about protecting privacy and the principles applicable to recording in public places (GDPR).

---
## Ending

### Course summary

If you do not think you are ready to take the test, you can:

- undergo the training one more time;
- look through the key points of the course in the form of a summary (see below);
- familiarize yourself with [FAQ regarding drones](https://ulc.gov.pl/pl/drony/informacje-ogolne/czesto-zadawane-pytania-faq) (website of the Civil Aviation Authority).

**Frequently asked questions:**

1. Maintain particular caution in flight, think, anticipate and avoid situations which can be dangerous to other aircraft, uninvolved persons, animals, yourself (remote pilot) and the observer.
2. Plan your flight using the [KSID](https://uav.pansa.pl/) mission creator (if required in that location), and before starting the flight, report your intention in the [Drone Tower](https://www.pansa.pl/dronetower) mobile app.
3. Check the weather forecast – as early as at the stage of planning your flight.
4. Do not create threats to air traffic – **manned aircraft always have priority**.
5. Never fly near aerodromes without a permit. When you have already received such a permit, always bear in mind the ongoing air traffic. The permit issuer will not suspend the traffic for the duration of your flight!!
6. Do not create threats to protected installations, facilities, areas – evade them as early as at the stage of planning your flight path.
7. Avoid assemblies of people, do not disturb mass events.
8. Do not forget about the GDPR – if you take photographs or shoot films, it means that you collect personal data.
9. Remember about the environment and permitted noise level.
10. Remember, you – as a remote pilot – bear criminal responsibility for the flight (even if you are accompanied by an observer).
11. Fly your drone in compliance with the rules, but also in accordance with the manufacturer recommendations – do not ignore the instruction manual of your UAV. Read it and always apply the information therein.
12. Prior to the flight, check the technical condition of the UAV and the entire UAV system.
13. Operate only UAVs and accessories which are in working order.
14. In the event of failure or communication problems, do not panic – track your UAV visually, the automatic mode should kick in.
15. If you conduct a mission in zones with restrictions for drones, you must hold relevant permissions and permits.

### Glossary of UAV-related terms

**AGL**
Height above ground level.

**AMSL**
Height above mean sea level

**ARC-b**
An airspace class with an increased risk of a collision in air traffic between a UAV and a manned aircraft.

**ATC**
Air Traffic Control

**ATZ**
Aerodrome Traffic Zone. Within an active ATZ, UAV flights are possible with the permission of and under conditions established by the ATZ operator.

**UAV**
An Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (a.k.a. RPAS, UAS, drone) used for conducting an air operation involving a UAV.

**BVLOS** (Beyond Visual Line of Sight)
Air operations involving a UAV, during which the pilot of an unmanned aerial vehicle beyond visual line of sight does not maintain direct visual contact with the UAV.

**CTR**
An airspace type wherein an air traffic controller (ATC) is responsible for air traffic safety. No flight may be performed without an ATC clearance, except for situations defined in aviation law. In a CTR zone, a remote pilot is under absolute obligation to give way to all manned aircraft and fly in such a way as to prevent any proximity with other aircraft. **The air traffic controller makes a decision on permitting an unmanned operation based on remote pilot’s declarations. The remote pilot shall be responsible for the unmanned flight safety.**

**DETECT AND AVOID**
A system for detecting and avoiding aircraft.

**DRA-P** (Drone Airspace Prohibited)
A prohibited zone where unmanned aerial system operations may not be performed, except for operations conducted under conditions defined by PANSA by authorised entities.

**DRA-R** (Drone Airspace Restricted)
A zone restricted for unmanned aerial systems in which UAS operations may be conducted with the permission of and under conditions established by PANSA or an authorised entity at the request of which the geographical zone has been designated.

**DRA-RH** (Drone Airspace Restricted High)
A zone restricted for unmanned aerial systems where the likelihood of obtaining permission for the aforementioned operations is high;

**DRA-RM** (Drone Airspace Restricted Medium)
A zone restricted for unmanned aerial systems where the likelihood of obtaining permission for the aforementioned medium;

**DRA-RL** (Drone Airspace Restricted Low)
A zone restricted for unmanned aerial systems where the likelihood of obtaining permission for the aforementioned operations is low.

**DRA-I** (Drone Airspace Information)
An information zone for unmanned aerial vehicles which contains information for ensuring the safety of operations conducted with unmanned aerial systems, including navigation warnings.

**DRA-T** (Drone Airspace Technical)
A zone restricted for unmanned aerial systems in which PANSA indicates additional technical requirements that must be met by the UAS with which the operation concerned is to be conducted.

**DRA-U** (Drone Airspace U-Space)
A geographical zone for unmanned aerial vehicles where UAS operations may take place only with the support of verified services provided in this zone and under conditions indicated by PANSA.

**EASA**
European Union Aviation Safety Agency

**FAIL SAFE**
It is an emergency mode advising what a UAV will do in the event of loss of link or signal between the radio transmitter and receiver.

**FIS**
Flight Information Service. FIS area of activity is the entire uncontrolled airspace.

**G**
An uncontrolled airspace class where air traffic control (ATC) IS NOT provided. However, FIS services are. As in the entire airspace, a remote pilot is obliged to give way to all manned aircraft and fly in such a way to prevent any proximity with other aircraft. It must be remembered that the G class airspace can contain airspace structures and geographical zones, where the rules of entry are defined by the operator of a given airspace structure or geographical zone.

**GEOFENCING**
It is an advanced technological system which provides for accurate designation of spatial boundaries within which UAVs may or may not fly by applying a UAV position tracking technology.

**MCTR** (Military CTR)
A controlled zone of a military aerodrome. UAV flights are possible only with the permission or for purposes of the operator of the zone concerned and on the conditions defined by the zone operator.

**MTMA** (Military TMA)
A controlled area of a military aerodrome or a hub of military aerodromes. UAV flights are prohibited.

**MTOM** (Maximum Take-off Mass)
It is the maximum mass of an unmanned aerial vehicle, including payload and fuel, up to which the unmanned aerial vehicle can be safely operated.

**TMA** (Terminal Control Area)
A controlled area of an aerodrome or aerodromes. UAV flights are prohibited.

**TRA** (Temporary Reserved Area)
UAV flights in an active TRA zone are possible only with the permission of the zone operator.

**TSA** (Temporary Segregated Area)
UAV flights are prohibited in an active TSA zone.

**TWR**
The so-called tower, an ATC controller.

**VLOS** (Visual Line of Sight)
Operation involving the use of a UAV, where a remote pilot or at least one observer maintains continuous unaided visual contact with the unmanned aerial vehicle to establish its position in relation to the remote pilot and airspace, and to ensure a safe distance from other aircraft, obstacles, persons or animals.
